questions.question: A radiograph shows crisply drawn edges everywhere, yet the whole image is a uniform gray with almost no density differences. Which pillar of sensitivity failed?
options: Definition - the edges are not sharp enough
Radiographic contrast - there is too little density difference between areas
Film graininess - the grains are too coarse
Geometric unsharpness - the source was too large
questions.explanation: Sensitivity needs both contrast and definition. Sharp edges with no density difference means the contrast side of the technique - energy, scatter control, film, or density - failed.

questions.question: Which variable changes subject contrast but has essentially no effect on film contrast?
options: Developer temperature
Film class (grain size)
Radiation energy (kV or isotope choice)
The density aim point on the characteristic curve
questions.explanation: Energy controls how differently the part's thick and thin sections transmit radiation - pure subject contrast. Developer, film class, and density operate on the film side.

questions.question: Scattered radiation reaching the film primarily:
options: Increases subject contrast by adding exposure
Sharpens edges by filling in the penumbra
Adds non-image fog that fills in density differences, lowering subject contrast
Raises film contrast by moving density up the characteristic curve
questions.explanation: Scatter carries no image information - it lays a uniform veil over the film that shrinks the density differences the primary beam created. Masking, collimation, and filtration exist to fight it.

questions.question: A tapered casting runs from 0.5 in. to 1.5 in. thick and must be covered in one exposure. Which approach puts the whole range inside the acceptable density window?
options: Lower the kV and lengthen the exposure
Raise the energy for latitude, or load two films of different speeds in one cassette
Use a finer-grain film at the same kV
Cut the source-to-film distance in half
questions.explanation: Higher energy flattens transmission differences and widens latitude; the double-film technique instead keeps contrast by letting a fast film record the thick end and a slow film the thin end.

questions.question: An area of a radiograph measures density 2.0. What fraction of the illuminator's light passes through it?
questions.explanation: Density is logarithmic: D = log10(I0/It), so D = 2.0 passes 1 part in 10^2, or 1% of the viewing light.

questions.question: A 0.100 in. focal spot sits 20 in. from the source side of the part, and the film is 0.5 in. behind that surface. The geometric unsharpness is:
questions.explanation: Ug = F × t / d = 0.100 × 0.5 / 20 = 0.0025 in. - well inside typical code limits.

questions.question: Which set of changes ALL act to improve image definition?
options: Smaller source, longer source-to-object distance, film tight against the part
Larger source, shorter source-to-film distance, faster film
Higher energy, fluorescent screens, shorter source-to-object distance
Higher mA, longer development, thicker shims
questions.explanation: Each term in Ug = F × t / d improves: smaller F, larger d, smaller t. The other sets either grow the penumbra or add graininess and mottle.

questions.question: Fluorescent intensifying screens are rarely permitted for high-sensitivity code radiography because:
options: They cannot intensify gamma radiation at all
Their large speed advantage comes with quantum mottle and lost definition
They push film density above the code maximum
They only function below 100 kV
questions.explanation: Fluorescent screens build the image from relatively few absorbed photons and spread light sideways in the phosphor - the resulting mottle and unsharpness defeat the sensitivity the code demands.

questions.question: Which statement about wire-type IQIs is correct?
options: The wires are placed parallel to the weld axis on the film side by default
The set is laid across the weld and the designated essential wire must be clearly visible in the area of interest
All six wires must be visible on every acceptable radiograph
Wire IQIs are used to verify film density instead of a densitometer
questions.explanation: Wire sets lie on the source side with wires crossing the weld; the procedure designates an essential wire, and that wire must show across the area of interest for the technique to pass.

questions.question: The source side of a closed vessel weld is inaccessible, so the IQI is placed on the cassette side. The radiograph must also show:
options: Nothing extra - film-side placement is the standard position
A lead letter B near the IQI
A lead letter F adjacent to the IQI so the interpreter knows the placement
Two IQIs stacked on top of each other
questions.explanation: Film-side IQIs image more easily than source-side ones, so codes require a lead "F" beside the IQI to flag the placement. The lead letter "B" is the backscatter check on the back of the cassette.
